problem_statement.Title: separability_matrix incorrect for nested CompoundModel due to _cstack bottom-right assignment
problem_statement.Description: When computing separability for CompoundModel trees that use the '&' operator, nested subtrees that return a coord_matrix ndarray are not copied correctly into the bottom-right block of the composed coord_matrix. The _cstack operator (astropy/modeling/separable.py) assigns the scalar 1 into the bottom-right slice when right is an ndarray instead of copying the ndarray. This causes nested CompoundModel separability to differ from the equivalent flattened expression (example: Pix2Sky_TAN() & (Linear1D(10) & Linear1D(5)) gives a different matrix than Pix2Sky_TAN() & Linear1D(10) & Linear1D(5)).
problem_statement.RootCause: In astropy/modeling/separable.py::_cstack, when the right operand is an ndarray (i.e., the coord matrix produced by a nested subtree), the code fills the bottom-right block with the scalar 1 (cright[-right.shape[0]:, -right.shape[1]:] = 1) instead of copying the right ndarray into that slice. This loses the nested subtree's 0/1 dependency pattern and forces the block to indicate every output depends on every input, producing incorrect separability for nested '&' compound models. Note: models that override Model._calculate_separability_matrix() return a matrix directly and thus bypass this composition logic.
StepsToReproduce: from astropy.modeling import models as m
separability_matrix(m.Pix2Sky_TAN() & m.Linear1D(10) & m.Linear1D(5))  # expected: flattened pattern
separability_matrix(m.Pix2Sky_TAN() & cm)  # observed: bottom-right block is all True (incorrect)
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Nested CompoundModels composed with '&' should produce the same separability matrix as the equivalent flattened expression. When a subtree produces a coord_matrix ndarray, that ndarray must be copied into the corresponding block of the composed coord_matrix, preserving its 0/1 dependency pattern.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior:
When the right subtree is a nested CompoundModel (thus producing an ndarray), the bottom-right block of the composed coord_matrix is filled with scalar 1s, resulting in an incorrect separability matrix that indicates non-separable dependencies for outputs that are in fact independent. Example (reported):
separability_matrix(m.Pix2Sky_TAN() & cm)
  bottom-right 2x2 block appears all True instead of preserving the nested 2x2 pattern.
problem_statement.Suggestions:
1) Fix astropy/modeling/separable.py::_cstack: when right is an ndarray, copy the ndarray into the bottom-right slice instead of assigning scalar 1, and preserve dtype when allocating cright (mirror how left ndarray is handled).
   - Exact change: in _cstack replace:
       cright[-right.shape[0]:, -right.shape[1]:] = 1
     with:
       cright = np.zeros((noutp, right.shape[1]), dtype=right.dtype)
       cright[-right.shape[0]:, -right.shape[1]:] = right
   - File and method to edit: astropy/modeling/separable.py::_cstack

 2) Add a regression test to astropy/modeling/tests/test_separable.py named test_nested_compound_equivalence that asserts nested CompoundModel gives the same separability_matrix as the equivalent flattened expression (include both the simple 2x2 diagonal check for Linear1D & Linear1D and the nested vs flattened Pix2Sky_TAN() example). Suggested test name and location: astropy/modeling/tests/test_separable.py::test_nested_compound_equivalence.

 3) Run the modeling test suite (pytest astropy/modeling/tests) to ensure the change produces no other regressions. Note: because transform._calculate_separability_matrix() can be implemented by specific models to provide custom matrices, those models bypass the composition logic and are unaffected.

patch:
diff --git a/astropy/io/ascii/rst.py b/astropy/io/ascii/rst.py
--- a/astropy/io/ascii/rst.py
+++ b/astropy/io/ascii/rst.py
@@ -27,7 +27,6 @@ def get_fixedwidth_params(self, line):
 
 
 class SimpleRSTData(FixedWidthData):
-    start_line = 3
     end_line = -1
     splitter_class = FixedWidthTwoLineDataSplitter
 
@@ -39,12 +38,29 @@ class RST(FixedWidth):
 
     Example::
 
-        ==== ===== ======
-        Col1  Col2  Col3
-        ==== ===== ======
-          1    2.3  Hello
-          2    4.5  Worlds
-        ==== ===== ======
+      >>> from astropy.table import QTable
+      >>> import astropy.units as u
+      >>> import sys
+      >>> tbl = QTable({"wave": [350, 950] * u.nm, "response": [0.7, 1.2] * u.count})
+      >>> tbl.write(sys.stdout,  format="ascii.rst")
+      ===== ========
+       wave response
+      ===== ========
+      350.0      0.7
+      950.0      1.2
+      ===== ========
+
+    Like other fixed-width formats, when writing a table you can provide ``header_rows``
+    to specify a list of table rows to output as the header.  For example::
+
+      >>> tbl.write(sys.stdout,  format="ascii.rst", header_rows=['name', 'unit'])
+      ===== ========
+       wave response
+         nm       ct
+      ===== ========
+      350.0      0.7
+      950.0      1.2
+      ===== ========
 
     Currently there is no support for reading tables which utilize continuation lines,
     or for ones which define column spans through the use of an additional
@@ -57,10 +73,15 @@ class RST(FixedWidth):
     data_class = SimpleRSTData
     header_class = SimpleRSTHeader
 
-    def __init__(self):
-        super().__init__(delimiter_pad=None, bookend=False)
+    def __init__(self, header_rows=None):
+        super().__init__(delimiter_pad=None, bookend=False, header_rows=header_rows)
 
     def write(self, lines):
         lines = super().write(lines)
-        lines = [lines[1]] + lines + [lines[1]]
+        idx = len(self.header.header_rows)
+        lines = [lines[idx]] + lines + [lines[idx]]
         return lines
+
+    def read(self, table):
+        self.data.start_line = 2 + len(self.header.header_rows)
+        return super().read(table)

 from io import StringIO
 
+import numpy as np
+
+import astropy.units as u
 from astropy.io import ascii
+from astropy.table import QTable
 
 from .common import assert_almost_equal, assert_equal
 
@@ -185,3 +189,27 @@ def test_write_normal():
 ==== ========= ==== ====
 """,
     )
+
+
+def test_rst_with_header_rows():
+    """Round-trip a table with header_rows specified"""
+    lines = [
+        "======= ======== ====",

+    assert out.getvalue().splitlines() == lines

problem_statement.Title: ascii.rst writer: TypeError on header_rows and incorrect border placement for multi-row headers
problem_statement.Description: When calling the reStructuredText (ascii.rst) writer with header_rows provided (for example header_rows=["name","unit"]) the writer raises a TypeError because RST.__init__ does not accept header_rows and therefore the writer factory (_get_writer) cannot instantiate the RST writer. In addition, RST.write currently assumes the border/position line is always at index 1 in the list of lines returned by the base fixed-width writer; that index is only correct for the default single header row and will produce incorrect outer borders for multi-row headers. Tests covering ascii.rst writing and round-trip read/write with header_rows are missing.
problem_statement.RootCause: 1) RST.__init__ does not accept a header_rows parameter; core._get_writer builds writer_kwargs from user kwargs and passes header_rows to Writer(**writer_kwargs). Because RST.__init__ has no header_rows parameter Python raises TypeError. 2) RST.write uses a hard-coded lines[1] to pick the border/position line. FixedWidthData.write produces header rows first and then the optional position_line; the position_line is therefore located at index len(header_rows) in the output from the base writer. RST.write's fixed index works only when header_rows length == 1. 3) SimpleRSTData.start_line is a class attribute (3). FixedWidth.__init__ sets data.start_line only if it is None, so FixedWidth will not automatically adapt data.start_line for RST if header_rows is explicitly supplied. This means a test-driven decision is required whether RST should explicitly adjust data.start_line when the caller passes header_rows.
StepsToReproduce: Run the Python snippet from the original report:
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: The ascii.rst writer should accept header_rows (default None) and forward it to FixedWidth so header.header_rows and data.header_rows are set consistently. When header_rows is provided (e.g., ['name','unit']), the writer should produce a valid reStructuredText simple-table with the header name and unit rows and with the outer top and bottom border lines placed at the correct positions relative to the header rows. No TypeError should be raised.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Calling tbl.write(..., format='ascii.rst', header_rows=[...]) raises TypeError: RST.__init__() got an unexpected keyword argument 'header_rows'. Even if that TypeError is fixed, the current RST.write may place border lines incorrectly when header_rows length != 1 because it assumes lines[1] is the position/border line returned by the base writer.
problem_statement.Suggestions: 1) In astropy/io/ascii/rst.py modify RST.__init__ to accept header_rows=None and forward header_rows to the FixedWidth base class (i.e., call super().__init__(..., header_rows=header_rows)). 2) In astropy/io/ascii/rst.py modify RST.write so it determines the correct position/border line dynamically from the header object (header.header_rows and header.position_line) rather than using lines[1]. Concretely: after lines = super().write(lines), compute pos_index = len(getattr(self.header, 'header_rows', ['name'])) if self.header.position_line is not None (with bounds checking), use lines[pos_index] as the border, and prepend/append that computed border line. 3) Add unit tests in astropy/io/ascii/tests/test_rst.py that exercise writing and read/write round-trips with header_rows=['name','unit'] (including a QTable + units example) and assert the produced reStructuredText simple-table matches the expected layout. 4) Because SimpleRSTData.start_line is a fixed class attribute (3), FixedWidth.__init__ will not override it; if tests reveal misalignment for non-default header_rows, then update RST.__init__ to set self.data.start_line appropriately when header_rows is explicitly provided (for example to len(header_rows) + (1 if header.position_line is not None else 0)). This last step should be driven by the unit tests to preserve current default behavior when header_rows is not explicitly supplied.

patch:
diff --git a/astropy/io/ascii/qdp.py b/astropy/io/ascii/qdp.py
--- a/astropy/io/ascii/qdp.py
+++ b/astropy/io/ascii/qdp.py
@@ -68,7 +68,7 @@ def _line_type(line, delimiter=None):
     _new_re = rf"NO({sep}NO)+"
     _data_re = rf"({_decimal_re}|NO|[-+]?nan)({sep}({_decimal_re}|NO|[-+]?nan))*)"
     _type_re = rf"^\s*((?P<command>{_command_re})|(?P<new>{_new_re})|(?P<data>{_data_re})?\s*(\!(?P<comment>.*))?\s*$"
-    _line_type_re = re.compile(_type_re)
+    _line_type_re = re.compile(_type_re, re.IGNORECASE)
     line = line.strip()
     if not line:
         return "comment"
@@ -306,7 +306,7 @@ def _get_tables_from_qdp_file(qdp_file, input_colnames=None, delimiter=None):
 
             values = []
             for v in line.split(delimiter):
-                if v == "NO":
+                if v.upper() == "NO":
                     values.append(np.ma.masked)
                 else:
                     # Understand if number is int or float

     assert np.isclose(table2["MJD_nerr"][0], -2.37847222222222e-05)

     example_qdp = """
     ! Swift/XRT hardness ratio of trigger: XXXX, name: BUBU X-2
     ! Columns are as labelled
@@ -70,6 +81,8 @@ def test_roundtrip(tmp_path):
     53000.123456 2.37847222222222e-05    -2.37847222222222e-05   -0.292553       -0.374935
     NO 1.14467592592593e-05    -1.14467592592593e-05   0.000000        NO
     """
+    if lowercase:
+        example_qdp = lowercase_header(example_qdp)
 
     path = str(tmp_path / "test.qdp")
     path2 = str(tmp_path / "test2.qdp")

problem_statement.Title: ascii.qdp: QDP reader should accept case-insensitive commands (e.g. 'read serr')
problem_statement.Description: The QDP ASCII reader (astropy.io.ascii.qdp) treats QDP command lines (for example "READ SERR 1 2") as case-sensitive. The line-classification regex in _line_type only matches uppercase commands, so lowercase or mixed-case commands such as "read serr 1 2" are not recognized and raise ValueError: "Unrecognized QDP line: read serr 1 2". The downstream code already canonicalizes command tokens (e.g. command[1].lower()), so the core issue is case-sensitive detection of command lines. Separately, the per-token masked-value detection (if v == "NO") in _get_tables_from_qdp_file is an exact, case-sensitive comparison and will miss lowercase 'no' tokens if present.
problem_statement.RootCause: In astropy/io/ascii/qdp.py the combined line-type regex used by _line_type contains literal uppercase command tokens (e.g. _command_re = r"READ [TS]ERR(\s+[0-9]+)+") and is compiled without case-insensitive flags (re.compile(_type_re)). Therefore lines like "read serr 1 2" do not match the 'command' branch of the regex and are treated as unrecognized. Separately, masked-value detection in _get_tables_from_qdp_file uses an exact check (v == "NO"), so lowercase 'no' is not treated as a masked value.
StepsToReproduce: Create a file test.qdp with contents:
Observe ValueError: Unrecognized QDP line: read serr 1 2
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: Table.read(..., format='ascii.qdp') should accept QDP command lines regardless of case (e.g. 'READ SERR', 'read serr', 'Read Serr') and parse the error columns. Optionally, data tokens representing masked values (e.g. 'NO') should be recognized case-insensitively if project policy prefers that.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Lowercase or mixed-case QDP command lines (e.g. 'read serr 1 2') are not recognized by the reader and cause a ValueError: 'Unrecognized QDP line: read serr 1 2'.
problem_statement.Suggestions:
Recommended changes and tests (explicit, actionable):

1) Make the command-line classification case-insensitive (minimal local change):
   - Edit function astropy.io.ascii.qdp._line_type: where the combined _type_re is compiled, compile it with the re.IGNORECASE flag. Specifically, change the compile call within _line_type from re.compile(_type_re) to re.compile(_type_re, re.IGNORECASE).
   - Rationale: this allows 'READ SERR', 'read serr', 'Read Serr', etc. to match as command lines. Downstream code already lowercases command[1] (err_specs[command[1].lower()]), so the rest of the pipeline will operate correctly once the line is recognized.

2) Make masked-value detection consistent and tolerant (optional but recommended if input files may use lowercase 'no'):
   - Edit function astropy.io.ascii.qdp._get_tables_from_qdp_file: in the value-parsing loop, normalize tokens before comparison. Replace the literal check if v == "NO" with a normalized check, e.g., v_stripped = v.strip(); if v_stripped.upper() == "NO": values.append(np.ma.masked)
   - Also when attempting numeric conversions, use v_stripped to pass to int/float conversions.
   - Rationale: if the line-type regex is made case-insensitive, lines like 'no no no' may be classified differently; normalizing value tokens keeps parsing consistent and accepts lowercase 'no' as a masked marker.

3) Add unit tests to prevent regressions:
   - In astropy/io/ascii/tests/test_qdp.py, add a test that uses lowercase/mixed-case command lines and verifies parsing succeeds and error columns are produced. Example test: read text 'read serr 1 2\n1 0.5 1 0.5\n' via Table.read(..., format='ascii.qdp') or _read_table_qdp and assert the table has expected columns and types and that no ValueError is raised.
   - If you accept lowercase 'no', add a test with lowercase 'no' tokens verifying they are read as masked values.

4) Update docstrings/comments as needed: add an example in the qdp.py docstring or tests that demonstrates that commands are case-insensitive.

5) Consider scope and optional broader change: core.fill_values in core._set_fill_values / core._set_masks uses exact string matching; if you prefer project-wide case-insensitive fill-value handling, coordinate a separate change in core (this is a larger-scope change and should be considered separately). For a local fix in the QDP reader, updating _get_tables_from_qdp_file is sufficient.

A project member will respond to you as soon as possible; in the meantime, please double-check the [guidelines for submitting issues](https://github.com/astropy/astropy/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md#reporting-issues) and make sure you've provided the requested details.

GitHub issues in the Astropy repository are used to track bug reports and feature requests; If your issue poses a question about how to use Astropy, please instead raise your question in the [Astropy Discourse user forum](https://community.openastronomy.org/c/astropy/8) and close this issue.

If you feel that this issue has not been responded to in a timely manner, please send a message directly to the [development mailing list](http://groups.google.com/group/astropy-dev).  If the issue is urgent or sensitive in nature (e.g., a security vulnerability) please send an e-mail directly to the private e-mail feedback@astropy.org.
Huh, so we do have this format... https://docs.astropy.org/en/stable/io/ascii/index.html

@taldcroft , you know anything about this?
This is the format I'm using, which has the issue: https://docs.astropy.org/en/stable/api/astropy.io.ascii.QDP.html

The issue is that the regex that searches for QDP commands is not case insensitive. 

This attached patch fixes the issue, but I'm sure there's a better way of doing it.

[qdp.patch](https://github.com/astropy/astropy/files/10667923/qdp.patch)

@jak574 - the fix is probably as simple as that. Would you like to put in a bugfix PR?

test_patch:
diff --git a/astropy/nddata/mixins/tests/test_ndarithmetic.py b/astropy/nddata/mixins/tests/test_ndarithmetic.py
--- a/astropy/nddata/mixins/tests/test_ndarithmetic.py
+++ b/astropy/nddata/mixins/tests/test_ndarithmetic.py
@@ -1310,3 +1310,42 @@ def test_raise_method_not_supported():
     # raise error for unsupported propagation operations:
     with pytest.raises(ValueError):
         ndd1.uncertainty.propagate(np.mod, ndd2, result, correlation)
+
+
+def test_nddata_bitmask_arithmetic():
+    # NDData.mask is usually assumed to be boolean, but could be
+    # a bitmask. Ensure bitmask works:
+    array = np.array([[0, 1, 0], [1, 0, 1], [0, 1, 0]])
+    mask = np.array([[0, 1, 64], [8, 0, 1], [2, 1, 0]])
+
+    nref_nomask = NDDataRef(array)
+    nref_masked = NDDataRef(array, mask=mask)
+
+    # multiply no mask by constant (no mask * no mask)
+    assert nref_nomask.multiply(1.0, handle_mask=np.bitwise_or).mask is None
+
+    # multiply no mask by itself (no mask * no mask)
+    assert nref_nomask.multiply(nref_nomask, handle_mask=np.bitwise_or).mask is None
+
+    # multiply masked by constant (mask * no mask)
+    np.testing.assert_equal(
+        nref_masked.multiply(1.0, handle_mask=np.bitwise_or).mask, mask
+    )
+
+    # multiply masked by itself (mask * mask)
+    np.testing.assert_equal(
+        nref_masked.multiply(nref_masked, handle_mask=np.bitwise_or).mask, mask
+    )
+
+    # multiply masked by no mask (mask * no mask)
+    np.testing.assert_equal(
+        nref_masked.multiply(nref_nomask, handle_mask=np.bitwise_or).mask, mask
+    )
+
+    # check bitwise logic still works
+    other_mask = np.array([[64, 1, 0], [2, 1, 0], [8, 0, 2]])
+    nref_mask_other = NDDataRef(array, mask=other_mask)
+    np.testing.assert_equal(
+        nref_mask_other.multiply(nref_masked, handle_mask=np.bitwise_or).mask,
+        np.bitwise_or(mask, other_mask),
+    )

problem_statement.Title: NDDataRef mask propagation in v5.3: handle_mask called with None causing TypeError
problem_statement.Description: In astropy v5.3 the mask propagation for NDDataRef arithmetic fails when using a callable handle_mask (for example a numpy ufunc like np.bitwise_or) and exactly one operand has a mask. The NDArithmeticMixin._arithmetic_mask implementation can call the provided handle_mask with one argument equal to None, which raises a TypeError (e.g. unsupported operand type(s) for |: 'int' and 'NoneType'). The intended behavior (and the behavior expected by existing tests) is that if only one operand has a mask that mask is copied to the result; handle_mask should only be invoked when both operands have non-None masks. This issue is observed when calling NDDataRef.multiply on an instance with a mask and a scalar or another NDDataRef without a mask using handle_mask=np.bitwise_or.
problem_statement.RootCause: NDArithmeticMixin._arithmetic_mask has an asymmetric conditional that fails to handle the case where self.mask is present and operand.mask is None. In that case the function reaches the final else branch and invokes handle_mask(self.mask, operand.mask) where operand.mask is None. For numpy ufuncs (e.g. np.bitwise_or) this results in a TypeError because they are not defined for None operands. The intended semantics (per docstring and tests) are: if only one mask is present, return a copy of that mask; only call handle_mask when both masks are present.
StepsToReproduce: python -c "import numpy as np; from astropy.nddata import NDDataRef; arr = np.array([[0,1,0],[1,0,1],[0,1,0]]); mask = np.array([[0,1,64],[8,0,1],[2,1,0]]); n_nomask = NDDataRef(arr); n_mask = NDDataRef(arr, mask=mask); n_mask.multiply(1., handle_mask=np.bitwise_or).mask"

python -c "import numpy as np; from astropy.nddata import NDDataRef; arr = np.array([[0,1,0],[1,0,1],[0,1,0]]); mask = np.array([[0,1,64],[8,0,1],[2,1,0]]); n_nomask = NDDataRef(arr); n_mask = NDDataRef(arr, mask=mask); n_mask.multiply(n_nomask, handle_mask=np.bitwise_or).mask"
Also try reversing operand order: n_nomask.multiply(n_mask, handle_mask=np.bitwise_or).mask
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: If only one operand has a mask, that mask should be deep-copied to the result and returned as result.mask. handle_mask (callable) should only be invoked when both operands provide non-None masks. No TypeError should be raised when handle_mask is a numpy ufunc and one operand lacks a mask.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: When calling arithmetic with handle_mask=np.bitwise_or and one operand lacking a mask, a TypeError is raised (e.g. unsupported operand type(s) for |: 'int' and 'NoneType') because handle_mask is called with a None mask argument.
problem_statement.Suggestions: 1) Modify NDArithmeticMixin._arithmetic_mask to explicitly implement all four relevant cases so that handle_mask is only called when both operands have non-None masks. Concretely: in NDArithmeticMixin._arithmetic_mask, (a) if handle_mask is None -> return None; (b) if operand is None (collapse) -> return deepcopy(self.mask); (c) if both masks are None -> return None; (d) if exactly one mask present -> return deepcopy(that mask); (e) if both masks present -> return handle_mask(mask_self, mask_operand, **kwds). 2) Add a focused regression test in astropy/nddata/mixins/tests/test_ndarithmetic.py (for example test_mask_propagation_with_one_operand_none) that verifies no TypeError is raised and that the resulting mask is a deep copy equal to the non-None mask for the following cases: (masked * scalar), (masked * nomask), and (nomask * masked). The test should exercise at least NDDataRef and NDDataArray (compat) and should try both np.bitwise_or and np.logical_or as handle_mask values to ensure behavior across numpy ufuncs and logical functions. 3) Consider applying the patch to maintenance branches if this is confirmed a regression from an earlier release.

hints_text:
Welcome to Astropy 👋 and thank you for your first issue!

A project member will respond to you as soon as possible; in the meantime, please double-check the [guidelines for submitting issues](https://github.com/astropy/astropy/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md#reporting-issues) and make sure you've provided the requested details.

GitHub issues in the Astropy repository are used to track bug reports and feature requests; If your issue poses a question about how to use Astropy, please instead raise your question in the [Astropy Discourse user forum](https://community.openastronomy.org/c/astropy/8) and close this issue.

If you feel that this issue has not been responded to in a timely manner, please send a message directly to the [development mailing list](http://groups.google.com/group/astropy-dev).  If the issue is urgent or sensitive in nature (e.g., a security vulnerability) please send an e-mail directly to the private e-mail feedback@astropy.org.
@bmorris3 , do you think this is related to that nddata feature you added in v5.3?
Hi @KathleenLabrie. I'm not sure this is a bug, because as far as I can tell the `mask` in NDData is assumed to be boolean: 

https://github.com/astropy/astropy/blob/83f6f002fb11853eacb689781d366be6aa170e0e/astropy/nddata/nddata.py#L51-L55

There are updates to the propagation logic in v5.3 that allow for more flexible and customizable mask propagation, see discussion in https://github.com/astropy/astropy/pull/14175.

You're using the `bitwise_or` operation, which is different from the default `logical_or` operation in important ways. I tested your example using `logical_or` and it worked as expected, with the caveat that your mask becomes booleans with `True` for non-zero initial mask values.
We are doing data reduction.  The nature of the "badness" of each pixel matters.  True or False does not cut it.  That why we need bits.  This is scientifically required.   A saturated pixel is different from a non-linear pixel, different from an unilliminated pixels, different .... etc. 

I don't see why a feature that had been there for a long time was removed without even a deprecation warning.
BTW, I still think that something is broken, eg.
```
>>> bmask = np.array([[True, False, False], [False, True, False], [False, False, True]])
>>> nref_bmask = NDDataRef(array, mask=bmask)
>>> nref_bmask.multiply(1.).mask
array([[True, None, None],
       [None, True, None],
       [None, None, True]], dtype=object)
```
Those `None`s should probably be `False`s not None's
There is *absolutely* a bug here. Here's a demonstration:

```
>>> data = np.arange(4).reshape(2,2)
>>> mask = np.array([[1, 0], [0, 1]]))
>>> nd1 = NDDataRef(data, mask=mask)
>>> nd2 = NDDataRef(data, mask=None)
>>> nd1.multiply(nd2, handle_mask=np.bitwise_or)
...Exception...
>>> nd2.multiply(nd1, handle_mask=np.bitwise_or)
NDDataRef([[0, 1],
           [4, 9]])
```

Multiplication is commutative and should still be here. In 5.2 the logic for arithmetic between two objects was that if one didn't have a `mask` or the `mask` was `None` then the output mask would be the `mask` of the other. That seems entirely sensible and I see no sensible argument for changing that. But in 5.3 the logic is that if the first operand has no mask then the output will be the mask of the second, but if the second operand has no mask then it sends both masks to the `handle_mask` function (instead of simply setting the output to the mask of the first as before).

Note that this has an unwanted effect *even if the masks are boolean*:
```
>>> bool_mask = mask.astype(bool)
>>> nd1 = NDDataRef(data, mask=bool_mask)
>>> nd2.multiply(nd1).mask
array([[False,  True],
       [ True, False]])
>>> nd1.multiply(nd2).mask
array([[None, True],
       [True, None]], dtype=object)
```
and, whoops, the `mask` isn't a nice happy numpy `bool` array anymore.

So it looks like somebody accidentally turned the lines

```
elif operand.mask is None:
            return deepcopy(self.mask)
```

into

```
elif operand is None:
            return deepcopy(self.mask)
```

@chris-simpson I agree that line you suggested above is the culprit, which was [changed here](https://github.com/astropy/astropy/commit/feeb716b7412c477c694648ee1e93be2c4a73700#diff-5057de973eaa1e5036a0bef89e618b1b03fd45a9c2952655abb656822f4ddc2aL458-R498). I've reverted that specific line in a local astropy branch and verified that the existing tests still pass, and the bitmask example from @KathleenLabrie works after that line is swapped. I'll make a PR to fix this today, with a new test to make sure that we don't break this again going forward. 
Many thanks for working on this, @bmorris3.

Regarding whether the `mask` is assumed to be Boolean, I had noticed in the past that some developers understood this to be the case, while others disagreed. When we discussed this back in 2016, however (as per the document you linked to in Slack), @eteq explained that the mask is just expected to be "truthy" in a NumPy sense of zero = False (unmasked) and non-zero = True (masked), which you'll see is consistent with the doc string you cited above, even if it's not entirely clear :slightly_frowning_face:.
Of course I think that flexibility is great, but I think intentional ambiguity in docs is risky when only one of the two cases is tested. 😬 
Indeed, I should probably have checked that there was a test for this upstream, since I was aware of some confusion; if only we could find more time to work on these important common bits that we depend on...

-            output_field.replace(encode_ascii('E'), encode_ascii('D'))

problem_statement.Title: io.fits: _scale_back_ascii no-op replace() call prevents 'E'->'D' exponent conversion
problem_statement.Description:
In astropy/io/fits/fitsrec.py, FITS_rec._scale_back_ascii builds ASCII representations of numeric values and attempts to replace the exponent separator 'E' with 'D' for floating-point ASCII output using:

    if 'D' in format:
        output_field.replace(encode_ascii('E'), encode_ascii('D'))

However, numpy.chararray.replace (and np.char.replace) return a new array rather than modifying the existing chararray in-place. The code calls replace() without assigning the returned array back into the existing buffer/view, so the substitution is a no-op and 'E' remains in the written ASCII output. encode_ascii('E') returns b'E', which is the correct argument type, but the replace result must be copied back into the field. The read-side code in the same file uses np.char.replace and assigns the result (dummy = np.char.replace(...)), indicating the intended functional usage pattern. The problem is isolated to FITS_rec._scale_back_ascii.
problem_statement.RootCause: The code assumes that numpy.chararray.replace (or np.char.replace) mutates the chararray in-place. In reality these functions return a new array; calling replace() without assigning the result does not alter output_field, so the intended 'E' -> 'D' substitutions never take effect when writing ASCII table fields.
StepsToReproduce: Create an ASCII FITS table (e.g., using astropy.io.fits.TableHDU or equivalent) with a floating-point column whose TFORM format indicates 'D' exponent notation (i.e., a 'D' format specifier).
Populate the column with a value that will produce an exponent when formatted (for example 1.23e4).
Write the table to an ASCII FITS file using astropy.io.fits.
Inspect the written file contents: the exponent separator should be 'D' (1.23D+04) for a 'D' format column, but with the current code it will remain 'E' (1.23E+04).
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When writing ASCII table columns whose format indicates 'D' exponent notation, the exponent separator characters in the written ASCII output should be 'D' (e.g., 1.23D+04).
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: The code attempts to replace 'E' with 'D' by calling output_field.replace(...) but does not assign the returned array back; because replace returns a new array, the underlying recarray buffer is not modified and exponent separators remain 'E'.
problem_statement.Suggestions:
Modify FITS_rec._scale_back_ascii to copy the result of np.char.replace back into the existing output_field buffer (slice assignment) so the substitution takes effect while preserving the recarray's backing buffer identity. Precisely: in astropy/io/fits/fitsrec.py, inside the FITS_rec._scale_back_ascii method, replace the lines:

    # Replace exponent separator in floating point numbers
    if 'D' in format:
        output_field.replace(encode_ascii('E'), encode_ascii('D'))

with:

    # Replace exponent separator in floating point numbers
    if 'D' in format:
        output_field[:] = np.char.replace(output_field,
                                         encode_ascii('E'),
                                         encode_ascii('D'))

Rationale: np.char.replace returns a new array; copying it into output_field[:] updates the existing recarray field buffer in-place (element-wise) and preserves the view/backing storage identity. Also add a unit test that creates an ASCII FITS table with a 'D' format floating-point column, writes it, and asserts that the written ASCII contains 'D' exponent separators (and that a read-back parse still yields the expected numeric values).

hints_text: It is tested with `astropy/io/fits/tests/test_checksum.py:test_ascii_table_data` but indeed the operation is not inplace and it does not fail. Using 'D' is probably better, but since #5362 (I had vague memory about something like this ^^, see also #5353) anyway 'D' and 'E' are read as double, so I think there is not difference on Astropy side.

patch:
diff --git a/astropy/wcs/wcs.py b/astropy/wcs/wcs.py
--- a/astropy/wcs/wcs.py
+++ b/astropy/wcs/wcs.py
@@ -1212,6 +1212,9 @@ def _array_converter(self, func, sky, *args, ra_dec_order=False):
         """
 
         def _return_list_of_arrays(axes, origin):
+            if any([x.size == 0 for x in axes]):
+                return axes
+
             try:
                 axes = np.broadcast_arrays(*axes)
             except ValueError:
@@ -1235,6 +1238,8 @@ def _return_single_array(xy, origin):
                 raise ValueError(
                     "When providing two arguments, the array must be "
                     "of shape (N, {0})".format(self.naxis))
+            if 0 in xy.shape:
+                return xy
             if ra_dec_order and sky == 'input':
                 xy = self._denormalize_sky(xy)
             result = func(xy, origin)
test_patch:
diff --git a/astropy/wcs/tests/test_wcs.py b/astropy/wcs/tests/test_wcs.py
--- a/astropy/wcs/tests/test_wcs.py
+++ b/astropy/wcs/tests/test_wcs.py
@@ -1093,3 +1093,21 @@ def test_keyedsip():
     assert isinstance( w.sip, wcs.Sip )
     assert w.sip.crpix[0] == 2048
     assert w.sip.crpix[1] == 1026
+
+
+def test_zero_size_input():
+    with fits.open(get_pkg_data_filename('data/sip.fits')) as f:
+        w = wcs.WCS(f[0].header)
+
+    inp = np.zeros((0, 2))
+    assert_array_equal(inp, w.all_pix2world(inp, 0))
+    assert_array_equal(inp, w.all_world2pix(inp, 0))
+
+    inp = [], [1]
+    result = w.all_pix2world([], [1], 0)
+    assert_array_equal(inp[0], result[0])
+    assert_array_equal(inp[1], result[1])
+
+    result = w.all_world2pix([], [1], 0)
+    assert_array_equal(inp[0], result[0])
+    assert_array_equal(inp[1], result[1])

problem_statement.Title: WCS transformations raise InconsistentAxisTypesError for zero-length inputs
problem_statement.Description: High-level WCS transformation methods (e.g. wcs_pix2world) raise an InconsistentAxisTypesError when called with empty coordinate inputs (zero-length lists/arrays). The traceback shows the error originates from the underlying wcslib C routines (wcsp2s / wcsp.s2p) because WCS._array_converter currently always calls into the C extension even when the prepared coordinate array has zero rows. The public Python dispatcher should instead short-circuit and return appropriately-shaped empty outputs without invoking the C extension, and iterative routines that perform reductions must also avoid reductions on empty arrays.
problem_statement.RootCause: WCS._array_converter's internal helpers (_return_list_of_arrays and _return_single_array) always call func(xy, origin) after preparing xy, even when xy.shape[0] == 0. Passing ncoord == 0 into wcslib's p2s/s2p triggers wcslib to raise InconsistentAxisTypesError. There is no short-circuit to return appropriately-shaped empty outputs in the Python layer. Additionally, _all_world2pix performs axis-1 reductions (np.sum, np.nanmax) on arrays that will be empty if upstream calls return early; therefore _all_world2pix must also detect and return early for empty pix0 to avoid empty-array reductions.
StepsToReproduce: Observed: InconsistentAxisTypesError raised from wcslib (ncoord==0). Expected: empty outputs.
problem_statement.ExpectedBehavior: When called with zero-length coordinate inputs, WCS transformation methods should return empty arrays (or lists/tuples of empty arrays) whose shapes match the broadcasted input shapes for the per-axis calling style, or (0, M) arrays where M is the number of output columns for the single-array calling style. For the ra/dec special-case, preserve the existing distinction: multi-argument calling style returns a tuple of two empty arrays shaped like the broadcasted input, while single-array calling style returns an empty (0,2) array.
problem_statement.ObservedBehavior: Calling wcs.wcs_pix2world([], [], 0) raises InconsistentAxisTypesError from wcslib because the Python dispatcher calls the C extension with ncoord == 0 (Wcsprm.p2s is invoked with an empty (0, M) array).
problem_statement.Suggestions:
Modify the Python-level dispatcher to detect zero-length inputs and return appropriately-shaped empty outputs without invoking the C extension, and add a guard in _all_world2pix to return early on empty initial approximations. Specifically:

- In astropy/wcs/wcs.py::WCS._array_converter, add short-circuit handling in both internal helper paths:
  - In _return_list_of_arrays(axes, origin): after broadcasting axes and constructing xy, but before calling func(xy, origin), if xy.shape[0] == 0 return empty arrays shaped like axes[0].shape for each output column; if ra_dec_order and sky == 'output', return a tuple of two empty arrays shaped axes[0].shape (preserving the ra/dec special-case return type). Use self.naxis as the number of output columns for the fallback.
  - In _return_single_array(xy, origin): before calling func, if xy.shape[0] == 0 return an empty array with shape (0, self.naxis) (or (0, 2) for ra_dec_order and sky == 'output').

- In astropy/wcs/wcs.py::WCS._all_world2pix, after computing pix0 = self.wcs_world2pix(world, origin) insert an early-return if pix0.shape[0] == 0 (return pix0 directly) to avoid np.sum/np.nanmax reductions on empty arrays.

- Add unit tests that exercise zero-length inputs for both calling conventions (list-of-arrays and single-array) and for all the public transformation methods (wcs_pix2world, all_pix2world, wcs_world2pix, all_world2pix, pix2foc, p4_pix2foc, det2im, sip_pix2foc, sip_foc2pix) covering ra_dec_order True/False cases. Tests should assert the returned shapes, types (float), and that no InconsistentAxisTypesError is raised.

- Audit direct uses of low-level wcslib wrappers (e.g., calls to w.wcs.p2s and w.wcs.s2p) and either: update such call sites to use the public WCS methods (which will be guarded by the above change), or add equivalent guards at those call sites. Direct calls to the C extension will still raise for zero-length inputs if left unchanged.
